rationales.A: Posterior STEMI is a STEMI equivalent and mandates emergent reperfusion. Treating as NSTE-ACS is the classic anti-pattern of missing posterior MI because there is no anterior ST elevation; this option would only be correct if anterior changes were purely reciprocal in a confirmed non-STEMI presentation with negative posterior leads (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
rationales.B: Primary PCI is preferred only when first medical contact to balloon is achievable within 120 minutes for transferred patients. With a 45 minute road transfer plus catheter-lab activation, that target will be breached. This would be the correct strategy if the PCI centre were <60 minutes away or onsite, in which case fibrinolysis is avoided (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
rationales.D: The 72 hour invasive window is the NSTE-ACS pathway and does not apply to STEMI equivalents. This would be correct only if posterior leads were negative and the troponin/ECG profile fitted intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS rather than transmural posterior infarction (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
rationales.E: Diagnosis is already established by V7 to V9 ST elevation and positive troponin; further serial ECGs add no information. The classic anti-pattern is treating posterior STEMI as an evolving NSTEMI; deliberate delay costs myocardium (time-is-muscle). Serial ECGs are appropriate only when initial ECG is non-diagnostic and troponin is pending (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).

rationales.B: The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is reserved for refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic or electrical instability, or mechanical complications. None apply here. This would be correct if she developed ongoing pain despite therapy, VT/VF, cardiogenic shock or acute mitral regurgitation (Heart Foundation/CSANZ NSTE-ACS).
rationales.C: The 72 hour window applies to intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS without dynamic changes or significant troponin rise. GRACE 158 plus dynamic ECG changes plus positive troponin places her in the high-risk group requiring intervention within 24 hours. This would be correct only if GRACE were 109 to 140 with no dynamic changes (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Selective/ischaemia-guided strategy is reserved for low-risk NSTE-ACS (GRACE <109, no dynamic changes, negative troponin). The anti-pattern is downgrading risk because of frailty or CKD; high-risk biomarker and ECG features override (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.E: CKD stage 3a is not a contraindication to angiography; high-risk NSTE-ACS with CKD has greater absolute benefit from early invasive management with contrast minimisation and peri-procedural hydration. KHA-CARI explicitly endorses angiography in this setting. This would only be correct in stage 5 CKD on conservative management with limited life expectancy (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; KHA-CARI).

rationales.A: Rate is already controlled at 78 with adequate beta-blockade and symptoms persist; tighter rate control has no mortality benefit and risks worsening fatigue/hypotension. This would be correct only if HR were >110 at rest with symptoms attributable to tachycardia, in lone AF without HFrEF (NHFA/CSANZ AF; CASTLE-AF).
rationales.B: Amiodarone is reasonable in HFrEF when flecainide/sotalol are contraindicated, but catheter ablation has superior survival, HF hospitalisation and quality-of-life outcomes in HFrEF + AF (CASTLE-AF). The anti-pattern is reflex amiodarone in HFrEF when ablation is feasible. Amiodarone would be the preferred rhythm option only if ablation is unavailable or patient declines (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).
rationales.C: Repeat DCCV without substrate modification is unlikely to give durable rhythm control after early relapse. This would be reasonable as a one-off bridge while planning ablation, or if a clearly reversible trigger (sepsis, thyrotoxicosis) has been corrected since the first DCCV (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).
rationales.D: AV node ablation plus pacing is a salvage strategy for refractory rate-control failure when rhythm control is not pursued, and commits to lifelong pacing without restoring sinus rhythm. It would be correct only after failed AF ablation and failed antiarrhythmics with persistent intolerable symptoms, particularly with pacing-dependent CRT indication (NHFA/CSANZ).

rationales.A: Rivaroxaban 20 mg requires CrCl >50 mL/min; the AMH dose for CrCl 15 to 49 mL/min is 15 mg daily, and AMH advises caution for CrCl <30. Continuing the standard dose risks bleeding from drug accumulation - a classic dose-overlooked-in-AKI anti-pattern. Continuing 20 mg is appropriate only if renal function recovers above CrCl 50 (AMH; NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.C: Dabigatran is ~80% renally cleared and is contraindicated in Australia when CrCl <30 mL/min per the AMH. eGFR 22 places him well below that threshold. Dabigatran would only be appropriate if eGFR were >=30 (>=50 for full 150 mg BD dose), and never in dialysis (AMH; NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Warfarin remains acceptable in advanced CKD but DOACs (especially apixaban) are now preferred where eGFR is >=15 because of lower intracranial haemorrhage and comparable stroke prevention. Warfarin would be the correct choice only for mechanical valves, moderate-severe mitral stenosis, dialysis dependence, or DOAC intolerance/contraindication (NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Left atrial appendage occlusion is reserved for absolute contraindication to long-term anticoagulation (recurrent major bleeding off therapy, prior intracerebral haemorrhage where rebleeding risk is unacceptable). He tolerates anticoagulation; only the agent/dose needs adjustment. LAAO would be correct only after a clear, persisting contraindication (NHFA/CSANZ).

rationales.B: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations but has no mortality benefit and is not foundational therapy; the anti-pattern is reaching for digoxin before completing the four pillars. Digoxin would be reasonable for persistent symptoms despite full GDMT, or for rate control in HFrEF with AF when beta-blocker is maximally tolerated (NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.C: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with resting HR >=70 despite the maximally tolerated beta-blocker, plus optimised baseline GDMT. He is HR 64 with un-uptitrated bisoprolol. This would be correct only after bisoprolol uptitration plateaus with persistent HR >=70 in sinus rhythm and persistent NYHA II to IV symptoms (NHFA/CSANZ; SHIFT).
rationales.D: Diuretics treat congestion but do not modify mortality; he is euvolaemic and the priority is mortality-reducing GDMT. The anti-pattern is escalating diuretics in a euvolaemic HFrEF patient because LVEF is poor. Diuretic escalation is correct only when there is congestion (rising weight, peripheral oedema, raised JVP) (NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.E: CRT/ICD is considered after >=3 months of optimal GDMT with persistent LVEF <=35% and appropriate QRS criteria (LBBB >=130 ms for CRT, structural cardiomyopathy meeting primary prevention ICD criteria). He has been on partial therapy for 2 weeks. Device referral now skips the reverse-remodelling chance from quadruple therapy (NHFA/CSANZ).

rationales.A: PARAGON-HF missed its primary endpoint in HFpEF; sacubitril/valsartan has only a Class IIb role and is positioned behind SGLT2 inhibitors. This would be a reasonable add-on if LVEF were in the lower-normal range (HFmrEF) or after SGLT2 inhibitor is established and symptoms persist (NHFA/CSANZ HF).
rationales.B: TOPCAT showed regional-subgroup-only benefit; spironolactone is a reasonable add-on but not first-line and SGLT2 inhibitors have stronger HFpEF evidence (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER). MRA would be the next step after SGLT2 inhibitor in symptomatic HFpEF, watching K and eGFR (NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.C: Beta-blockers do not have robust HFpEF mortality benefit and can worsen chronotropic incompetence (a key driver of exertional symptoms in HFpEF). They would be correct only for an independent indication (AF rate control, post-MI, symptomatic ischaemia) (NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Lifestyle measures (weight loss, CPAP, BP control) are essential adjuncts but withholding SGLT2 inhibitor misses a Class I-equivalent mortality/hospitalisation benefit. This combination alone would be correct only if SGLT2 inhibitors were contraindicated (recurrent DKA, severe genitourinary infection, eGFR <20 for initiation) (NHFA/CSANZ; EMPEROR-Preserved).

rationales.A: Routine morphine in acute heart failure is associated with increased mortality and ICU admission and is explicitly de-emphasised - a classic legacy anti-pattern. Low-flow nasal prongs cannot achieve adequate oxygenation at SpO2 86% on NRB. This option would not be correct in any modern APO scenario; morphine is reserved at most for terminal palliative dyspnoea (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular; NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.B: Immediate invasive ventilation overrides her Resuscitation Plan favouring non-invasive options; the 3CPO trial showed NIV frequently averts intubation in APO. Intubation would be correct only after a NIV trial fails (rising CO2, declining GCS, persistent hypoxia) or with frank cardiogenic shock requiring sedation/airway protection (Therapeutic Guidelines; NHFA/CSANZ).
rationales.C: IV beta-blocker in decompensated HF with cool peripheries and elevated lactate risks haemodynamic collapse; digoxin has no role in acute hypertensive APO. This would only be appropriate if rapid AF were the dominant driver of decompensation with preserved perfusion and afterload already addressed (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).
rationales.D: Nesiritide is not available in Australia (the anti-pattern of US-textbook recall). Inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension; this patient is hypertensive at 192/108 and needs afterload reduction, not inotropy. Inotropic support would be correct only with BP <90 systolic, evidence of end-organ hypoperfusion, and adequate filling (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).

rationales.A: Spontaneous type 1 Brugada with syncope of likely arrhythmic origin plus a family history of sudden death is the highest-risk phenotype, requiring definitive therapy not outpatient deferral. This pathway would be correct only for an asymptomatic incidental type 2/3 Brugada pattern without high-risk features or family history (CSANZ/HRS Brugada statement; BMJ Best Practice).
rationales.C: Flecainide is a Class IC sodium channel blocker that UNMASKS Brugada (it is the provocative challenge agent) and would precipitate ventricular arrhythmia. Flecainide is never correct in Brugada; the broader anti-pattern is reaching for sodium-channel blockers in any unexplained right-precordial ST elevation (CSANZ/HRS).
rationales.D: Amiodarone has no proven mortality benefit in Brugada and does not substitute for an ICD. Quinidine is the antiarrhythmic with the most Brugada-specific evidence and is used as ICD adjunct for recurrent shocks or storm. Amiodarone would be inappropriate even adjunctively (CSANZ/HRS).
rationales.E: Genetic testing informs cascade screening of relatives but ~70 to 80% of probands have no identified pathogenic SCN5A variant, so a negative result does not exclude Brugada and would not change management of a clinically diagnosed symptomatic patient. Genetic testing is appropriate concurrently, not as a delay to ICD (CSANZ/HRS).

rationales.A: Phaeochromocytoma screening has been done with normal urinary catecholamines and no paroxysmal symptoms; repeating is low-yield. Repeat metanephrines would be correct if she developed new paroxysmal headaches/palpitations/diaphoresis or an adrenal incidentaloma with imaging features suggestive of phaeo (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).
rationales.B: Renovascular hypertension has been excluded by Doppler and she lacks supportive features (abdominal bruit, flash pulmonary oedema, ACEi-induced AKI, asymmetric kidney sizes). MRA would be correct only if Doppler were equivocal in a high pre-test probability patient or fibromuscular dysplasia were suspected (younger woman) (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).
rationales.C: Cushing screening is justified by clinical features of hypercortisolism (central obesity, striae, easy bruising, proximal myopathy, glucose intolerance) - none described and HbA1c is normal. Dexamethasone suppression would be correct in resistant HTN plus Cushingoid features or recent rapid weight gain with new diabetes (Endocrine Society).
rationales.E: Empirical spironolactone is reasonable as fourth-line add-on in resistant HTN, but starting it before ARR will suppress the test for 4 to 6 weeks and miss a diagnosis with potentially curative implications (adrenalectomy in unilateral disease). This would be correct only after a primary aldosteronism workup is complete or declined (Endocrine Society).

rationales.B: Atropine acts on the AV node and frequently fails or paradoxically worsens infra-nodal (Mobitz II, wide-QRS) block by speeding atrial rate without improving His-Purkinje conduction. Atropine is a temporising bridge, not definitive. It would be correct only as initial pharmacotherapy for nodal (Mobitz I, narrow-QRS) bradycardia with reversible vagal trigger (ANZCOR ALS bradycardia algorithm; ESC).
rationales.C: Isoprenaline is an inpatient bridge to definitive pacing; discharging on chronotropic infusion is unsafe and ignores the Class I pacemaker indication. Isoprenaline use would be correct only as a short-term inpatient measure pending transvenous or permanent pacing (ANZCOR; ESC).
rationales.D: Perindopril does not cause AV block; no reversible cause has been identified. Watchful waiting misses the Class I indication for permanent pacing and risks progression to complete heart block with unreliable wide escape. Drug review would be the correct first step only if a culprit (e.g., beta-blocker, non-DHP calcium channel blocker, digoxin) was present (ESC).
rationales.E: AV node ablation deliberately CREATES complete heart block to control refractory tachyarrhythmias - the opposite of what is needed here. EP study is occasionally useful to localise block in fascicular disease with syncope, but the surface ECG is diagnostic. This option is a deliberate red herring exploiting the word ablation (ESC).

rationales.A: 1 mg adrenaline is the cardiac arrest dose; for symptomatic bradycardia ANZCOR recommends adrenaline INFUSION 2 to 10 micrograms/min or 20 to 50 microgram boluses. A 1 mg bolus risks hypertensive crisis, intracerebral haemorrhage and tachyarrhythmia. Immediate intubation is not indicated while perfusion can be restored by pacing. The 1 mg dose would only be correct in confirmed cardiac arrest (ANZCOR adult ALS).
rationales.B: Atropine is generally ineffective in CHB with a wide infra-nodal escape; ANZCOR explicitly recommends moving to pacing or chronotropic infusion. The anti-pattern is exhausting 3 mg atropine while the patient deteriorates. Atropine alone would be correct only as a brief trial in narrow-QRS (nodal) symptomatic bradycardia where AV node responsiveness is plausible (ANZCOR).
rationales.D: Fluid will not address the conduction problem and risks pulmonary oedema in an elderly patient. Restoring rate is the definitive treatment. Fluid bolus would be correct only if hypotension were clearly driven by volume depletion with a normal underlying conduction system (ANZCOR; Therapeutic Guidelines).
rationales.E: Calcium is for confirmed/suspected hyperkalaemia or calcium channel blocker/beta-blocker toxicity. K is 4.4 mmol/L and there is no toxicology history. Empirical calcium would be correct only with widened QRS plus peaked T waves of hyperkalaemia, dialysis miss, or known CCB overdose (ANZCOR; Therapeutic Guidelines).

rationales.A: Primary PCI is preferred only when first-medical-contact-to-balloon is within 120 minutes; beyond this window fibrinolysis with rescue PCI capability gives superior outcomes. This would be correct only if the PCI centre were reachable inside the 120 minute window from FMC (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS 2016 update).
rationales.C: STEMI diagnosis on ECG does not require troponin confirmation before reperfusion. The anti-pattern is waiting for biomarker confirmation in a clear STEMI; delaying therapy increases myocardial loss and mortality. Awaiting troponin is appropriate only when ECG is non-diagnostic and clinical picture is uncertain (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Anticoagulation alone is not a reperfusion strategy; STEMI requires fibrinolysis or PPCI. This is the classic anti-pattern of treating STEMI as if it were unstable angina. Heparin alone would be correct in NSTE-ACS while invasive management is being arranged within 24 to 72 hours (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Anti-ischaemic therapy without reperfusion is inadequate for STEMI; the small reciprocal change distraction is the anti-pattern (any reciprocal change confirms the STEMI). Beta-blockers acutely are also relatively contraindicated with incipient cardiogenic shock. Conservative therapy is only appropriate when patient is past the 12-hour window and is asymptomatic with no ongoing ischaemia (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

rationales.A: Discharge from the observe zone of the 0/1 hour algorithm is unsafe; she is neither low rule-out nor rule-in. The anti-pattern is treating a small delta as effectively negative. Discharge would be correct only with a low baseline value and absolute change below the rule-out cut-off (e.g., hs-cTnI baseline <5 ng/L with delta <2 ng/L) (ESC 0/1h algorithm; Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).
rationales.B: Rule-in on the 0/1 hour algorithm requires either an absolute value above the assay-specific rule-in cut-off or a 1-hour delta above the rule-in delta (>6 ng/L for hs-cTnI in this assay). Her delta is 4 ng/L and absolute is 18 ng/L. Diagnosing NSTEMI and committing to invasive angiography requires meeting one of these thresholds (ESC; Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Fibrinolysis is contraindicated in NSTE-ACS - it increases bleeding without improving outcomes. The anti-pattern is conflating high-risk-but-not-STEMI with STEMI. Fibrinolysis would be correct only with ST-elevation or STEMI equivalent and inability to deliver PPCI within 120 minutes (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Deferring testing for 2 weeks while in the observe zone risks missed evolving infarction; inpatient workup with repeat troponin and provocative or anatomical testing is required first. Outpatient exercise echo would be correct only after a confirmed rule-out with low-risk discharge pathway (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).

rationales.A: The 24-hour NSTEMI window does not apply when posterior leads confirm STEMI equivalent. This is the most common posterior STEMI miss - treating it as NSTEMI because there is only ST depression anteriorly. This pathway would be correct only with negative posterior leads and rise/fall biomarker pattern consistent with NSTEMI (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; ANZCOR).
rationales.C: Wellens syndrome shows biphasic or deeply inverted T waves in V2 to V3 in a pain-free patient with prior chest pain (a critical proximal LAD lesion sign); this ECG shows reciprocal ST depression with upright T and posterior ST elevation, not Wellens morphology. Wellens management is urgent inpatient angiography, not emergency PCI activation (LITFL; Heart Foundation).
rationales.D: RBBB shows an rSR pattern in V1 with a broad QRS (>=120 ms) and discordant T-wave inversion; this ECG has a narrow QRS with dominant R and upright T - the wrong morphology. The anti-pattern is anchoring on dominant R in V1 without checking QRS width (Heart Foundation; LITFL).
rationales.E: Digoxin effect shows scooped (Salvador Dali moustache) ST depression with shortened QT, typically in lateral leads, in patients on digoxin. He is not on digoxin and the pattern is acute ischaemic. This would only be correct in a digoxin-treated patient with the classic scooped morphology (AMH).

rationales.B: Clopidogrel is the alternative P2Y12 when ticagrelor and prasugrel are contraindicated (high bleeding risk, prior intracranial bleed, on long-term anticoagulation, ticagrelor dyspnoea intolerance). It is less potent than ticagrelor (PLATO showed superior outcomes for ticagrelor). This would be correct as default only when ticagrelor/prasugrel are contraindicated (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).
rationales.C: Single antiplatelet post-ACS-PCI substantially increases stent thrombosis risk; DAPT for at least 12 months is standard. This would be correct only after the planned DAPT duration is completed, or earlier if a major bleeding event mandates de-escalation (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Prasugrel is an alternative to ticagrelor but is contraindicated with prior stroke/TIA, not recommended over age 75, and at reduced dose if under 60 kg. Default ACS-PCI duration is 12 months, not 6 months. Six-month DAPT would be correct only with high bleeding risk after careful weighing (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).
rationales.E: DOAC plus aspirin is not standard post-ACS-PCI in sinus rhythm; this would be correct only if the patient had concurrent AF requiring anticoagulation, in which case triple therapy (DOAC + aspirin + clopidogrel) is given for the shortest possible period (typically 1 week to 1 month) then dual therapy with DOAC + clopidogrel (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

rationales.B: Fluid loading worsens pulmonary oedema in an already congested patient; IV beta-blockade in acute decompensation precipitates cardiogenic shock by removing residual sympathetic compensation. This combination would never be correct in ADHF; it would be appropriate only if the diagnosis were misread as septic tachyarrhythmia without congestion (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).
rationales.C: Routine opioid use in ADHF is associated with worse outcomes (increased mortality, ICU admission, intubation) and is no longer recommended - a key legacy anti-pattern. HFNO has not been shown superior to NIV in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. Morphine is reserved at most for terminal palliative dyspnoea (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension and end-organ hypoperfusion; this patient is hypertensive and not in shock. Chronic disease-modifying therapy (ACEi, beta-blocker) should generally be continued during decompensation unless hypotension or AKI dictates otherwise. Indefinite withdrawal worsens long-term outcomes (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Oral diuretic onset is too slow for acute severe pulmonary oedema with respiratory failure (gut oedema impairs absorption); IV diuretic is required. Oral diuresis is appropriate only in stable congestion managed in clinic or after acute response, not as initial management in respiratory distress (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).

rationales.B: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations but has no mortality benefit and is not a prognosis pillar; the anti-pattern is reaching for digoxin before completing four-pillar GDMT. Digoxin would be reasonable for persistent symptoms on full GDMT, particularly with concurrent AF requiring additional rate control (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; AMH).
rationales.C: Loop diuretics are symptom-controlling not prognosis-modifying; amlodipine is neutral in HFrEF but does not improve survival. The anti-pattern is escalating non-prognostic therapy while skipping the mortality-reducing pillars. Diuretic escalation is correct for symptomatic congestion, not asymptomatic LV dysfunction (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: CRT is indicated for LVEF <=35% with LBBB and QRS >=130 ms after at least 3 months of optimised GDMT; she has not had GDMT optimised first. CRT before four-pillar optimisation misses the reverse-remodelling benefit and risks inappropriate device implantation if LVEF recovers (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with HR >=77 bpm on maximally tolerated beta-blocker (SHIFT trial threshold); her HR is 64 bpm so she does not qualify. ARB substitutes for ACEi only when ACEi-intolerant (cough, angioedema), not as a routine switch. This option mixes two off-target choices (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; SHIFT).

rationales.A: In symptomatic AF of recent onset (<1 year), early rhythm control reduces cardiovascular events compared with rate control alone (EAST-AFNET 4). The 'wait for recurrences' approach is the pre-EAST-AFNET legacy practice. Rate-control-only would be correct in long-standing persistent AF (>1 year), asymptomatic patients, or patients who decline rhythm interventions (CSANZ AF Guideline 2018; EAST-AFNET 4).
rationales.C: She is symptomatic (EHRA IIb) and tachycardic at 118; anticoagulation alone is insufficient. Spontaneous reversion happens but should not be relied on. Anticoagulation alone would be appropriate only in asymptomatic permanent AF with rate already controlled (CSANZ AF).
rationales.D: Digoxin monotherapy is poor for exertional rate control (vagal action diminished by sympathetic activity) and her CHA2DS2-VA is 2 (age 65 to 74 = 1, female = 1, hypertension = 1 - actually 3), meeting the threshold for anticoagulation. The combined errors make this never correct (CSANZ AF).
rationales.E: Anticoagulation is required regardless of antiarrhythmic strategy when CHA2DS2-VA reaches threshold; rhythm control does not abolish stroke risk. Amiodarone is generally reserved for structural heart disease or failed first-line antiarrhythmics due to long-term toxicity (thyroid, lung, hepatic, ocular). This would only be appropriate in structural heart disease with failed flecainide/sotalol AND with anticoagulation (CSANZ AF; AMH).

rationales.B: She meets two apixaban dose-reduction criteria (weight 58 kg <=60 kg, and creatinine 138 micromol/L >=133 micromol/L) - any two of the three triggers the 2.5 mg BD dose. Standard 5 mg BD would lead to over-anticoagulation. This dose would be correct only with at most one reduction criterion (AMH; CSANZ AF).
rationales.C: Dabigatran is ~80% renally cleared and is contraindicated in Australia with CrCl <30 mL/min per AMH. eGFR 32 puts her at borderline and she lacks the renal margin apixaban provides. Dabigatran 150 mg BD would be appropriate only with stable CrCl >=50 mL/min in younger patients without bleeding risk (AMH; CSANZ AF).
rationales.D: Warfarin is acceptable but not preferred for non-valvular AF; DOACs (especially apixaban) have lower intracranial bleeding rates and avoid INR monitoring. Warfarin is the correct choice for mechanical valves, moderate-severe mitral stenosis, antiphospholipid syndrome, severe CKD/dialysis, or where DOACs are contraindicated/unavailable (CSANZ AF).
rationales.E: Dual antiplatelet therapy is inferior to anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF (ACTIVE-W trial showed this clearly). This is a key legacy anti-pattern of substituting antiplatelets for anticoagulation. DAPT for AF stroke prevention is essentially never correct (CSANZ AF; ACTIVE-W).

rationales.B: Both AS and HOCM produce systolic murmurs in the precordial/parasternal area; location alone does not discriminate. AS classically radiates to carotids and HOCM does not, but mere presence at the right upper sternal edge is not diagnostic (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.C: LVH with strain occurs in both severe AS (pressure overload) and HCM (intrinsic hypertrophy); ECG cannot reliably differentiate them. The anti-pattern is using a non-specific finding to claim discrimination. LVH on ECG only confirms hypertrophy is present, not its cause (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.D: Exertional syncope occurs in both severe AS and HOCM and is a high-risk red-flag in both; it cannot discriminate. Lead-pipe equivalence in symptoms is a classic teaching point for these two outflow obstructions (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
rationales.E: Pulsus parvus et tardus does favour AS over HOCM (HOCM gives a bisferiens spike-and-dome pulse), but the question asks the STRONGEST discriminator. Dynamic squat/Valsalva manoeuvres are more specific and reproducible at the bedside than pulse character, which can be obscured by stiff vessels in older patients. Pulse character is supportive but not the strongest single sign (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

rationales.A: Single long-acting bronchodilator (LAMA or LABA) is appropriate for low-symptom, low-exacerbation patients (mMRC <2, CAT <10, <1 exacerbation/year); he has both high symptom burden (mMRC 2, CAT 18) and 2 exacerbations in 12 months, mandating dual bronchodilation. This would be correct only as initial therapy in a less symptomatic, infrequent-exacerbator patient (Lung Foundation Australia COPD-X; GOLD).
rationales.C: ICS adds pneumonia risk and should be reserved for patients with blood eosinophils >=300 cells/microL, asthma-COPD overlap, or persistent exacerbations on dual bronchodilator therapy. His eosinophils are 120. This option is the classic 'reach for ICS too early' anti-pattern. LABA/ICS would be appropriate only with eosinophilic phenotype or asthma overlap (COPD-X; GOLD).
rationales.D: Triple therapy is escalation FROM dual bronchodilation when exacerbations persist; he has not trialled dual bronchodilator yet and lacks ICS-favouring features (eos <300, no asthma overlap). Triple therapy would be correct after dual bronchodilator failure with rising eosinophils or persistent exacerbations (COPD-X; GOLD).
rationales.E: Theophylline has a narrow therapeutic index, significant drug interactions (cytochrome P450), and limited efficacy; it is a last-line agent in stable COPD. Theophylline would only be considered after maximal inhaler therapy in patients with persistent symptoms and no other options (AMH; COPD-X).

rationales.B: Intubation is for failure of NIV, severe agitation precluding mask tolerance, copious secretions, or cardiopulmonary arrest. Maintaining excessive oxygen (8 L Hudson mask drives the acidosis higher via Haldane effect and loss of hypoxic drive) compounds the harm. Intubation is correct only after NIV trial fails or contraindications to NIV exist (COPD-X; BTS NIV).
rationales.C: Reducing oxygen alone will not correct the respiratory acidosis quickly enough in a drowsy patient with pH 7.21 and pCO2 88; NIV is needed to actively unload CO2. Observation alone would be correct only with milder hypercapnia (pH >7.35) and adequate respiratory effort (COPD-X; BTS NIV).
rationales.D: Doxapram is rarely used now and has been superseded by NIV; it causes agitation, arrhythmia, and seizure. Continued excessive oxygen compounds the problem. Doxapram has essentially no current role in COPD exacerbation management (AMH; BTS NIV).
rationales.E: Nasal high-flow oxygen can be used in selected COPD patients (HOT-AIR trial) but NIV (BiPAP) has stronger evidence and is first-line in hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidosis (pH <7.35). HFNO would be a reasonable second-line option if NIV is not tolerated, not the first-line choice with pH 7.21 (COPD-X; BTS NIV).

rationales.A: Mild to moderate asthma has PEF >60% of best, speaks in sentences, no accessory muscle use. Her PEF 39%, short-phrase speech, RR 30, SpO2 91% all meet severe criteria. The anti-pattern is under-classifying severity from a single number (e.g., looking only at SpO2). This management would only be appropriate in true mild-moderate exacerbation (Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.C: Life-threatening features are silent chest, cyanosis, bradycardia, exhaustion, confusion, or PEF <33% - she does not yet meet these. Pre-emptive intubation in severe (not life-threatening) asthma without therapeutic trial increases morbidity; bronchospasm is best treated medically while preparing for escalation. Intubation is correct only after failed maximal medical therapy or imminent respiratory arrest (Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.D: Discharge without observation in a severe exacerbation is unsafe; she requires intensive bronchodilation, systemic steroid, and reassessment over hours to confirm response (typically 4-hour observation post stabilisation). Oral prednisolone discharge is correct for mild-moderate exacerbation after good response to inhaled bronchodilator in ED (Australian Asthma Handbook).
rationales.E: IV magnesium 2 g over 20 minutes is a useful adjunct in severe asthma not responding to maximal first-line bronchodilation; it is not first-line. The anti-pattern is leading with adjunctive therapy. Magnesium is correct after 1 hour of continuous nebulised salbutamol, ipratropium, and systemic steroid with inadequate response (Australian Asthma Handbook; eTG Respiratory).

rationales.A: Age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 in patients >=50) is only valid in LOW pretest probability (Wells <=4, or 'PE unlikely'). Her Wells is 7.5 ('PE likely'), so D-dimer cannot be used as rule-out regardless of threshold. The anti-pattern is applying D-dimer to high-probability patients. Age-adjusted threshold is correct only in PE-unlikely patients (THANZ; BTS).
rationales.C: V/Q is correct when CTPA is contraindicated (severe contrast allergy, severe renal impairment, pregnancy where V/Q may be preferred). With normal renal function and no allergy, CTPA is first-line. V/Q would be the correct first choice only with contrast contraindication or in pregnancy with normal CXR (eTG; THANZ).
rationales.D: Delaying imaging in a high-probability patient is unsafe; PE mortality is concentrated in the first 24 hours. The anti-pattern is treating D-dimer as a screening test independent of pretest probability. Repeat D-dimer is essentially never correct in suspected acute PE (eTG; THANZ).
rationales.E: Lower-limb US is useful when chest imaging is contraindicated (e.g., pregnancy with negative initial workup) or to add evidence in equivocal cases. It is not first-line when PE is the principal clinical concern with high pretest probability; a positive US confirms VTE but a negative US does not exclude PE (eTG; THANZ).

rationales.B: Warfarin is no longer first-line for unprovoked PE without contraindications to DOACs; DOAC monotherapy avoids parenteral bridging and INR monitoring. Warfarin remains correct for antiphospholipid syndrome (triple positive), severe renal impairment, mechanical valves, or where DOACs are unavailable/contraindicated (THANZ; eTG).
rationales.C: Thrombolysis is reserved for high-risk (massive) PE with haemodynamic instability (sustained hypotension, shock) or selected intermediate-high-risk with deterioration. He is haemodynamically stable with no RV strain. Thrombolysis would only be correct with SBP <90 sustained, cardiac arrest, or progressive RV failure on therapeutic anticoagulation (eTG; THANZ).
rationales.D: IVC filters are reserved for absolute contraindications to anticoagulation or recurrent PE despite therapeutic anticoagulation. Recent long-haul flight is a transient provoking factor that justifies anticoagulation for 3 months but is not a contraindication. IVC filter would be correct only with active major bleeding precluding anticoagulation (eTG; THANZ).
rationales.E: Aspirin alone is inadequate for acute PE treatment; therapeutic anticoagulation for at least 3 months is mandatory. Aspirin has a role in extended secondary prevention AFTER initial 6-12 months of therapeutic anticoagulation in low-recurrence-risk patients (ASPIRE/WARFASA trials), not as initial treatment (eTG; THANZ).

rationales.A: His SMART-COP is high (multilobar + RR + oxygenation + albumin + SBP scores >=5), indicating high risk of needing intensive respiratory or vasopressor support. Oral therapy and general ward miss this. The anti-pattern is anchoring on CURB-65 (which can underestimate in younger patients). Oral amoxicillin + doxycycline is correct for low-severity CAP (SMART-COP 0-2, CURB-65 0-1) (eTG Respiratory).
rationales.C: Even if CURB-65 is reassuring at 1, his hypotension, hypoxia, multilobar disease, and biochemical derangements meet inpatient criteria. SMART-COP is more sensitive for need for intensive support in younger patients. Outpatient moxifloxacin would be correct only for low-severity CAP without inpatient indications (eTG Respiratory).
rationales.D: Benzylpenicillin monotherapy does not cover Mycoplasma, Legionella, or atypicals and is inadequate for severe CAP requiring ICU-level support. Benzylpenicillin can be used in confirmed pneumococcal pneumonia without atypical concern, but not as empirical therapy for severe CAP (eTG Respiratory).
rationales.E: Broad-spectrum antipseudomonal + MRSA cover is reserved for risk factors: structural lung disease (bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis), recent hospitalisation, recent broad-spectrum antibiotics, MRSA colonisation, or aspiration with structural disease. He has none of these. This combination would only be correct with documented risk factors or culture-directed therapy (eTG Respiratory).

rationales.B: She is therapeutically anticoagulated on apixaban (PE on apixaban is rare and would usually present with focal pleuritic pain/hypoxia), has no haemodynamic instability, and her picture is dominated by congestive features (raised JVP, B-lines, BNP >4000). D-dimer is non-specifically elevated in HF and AF and is not a rule-in test. This would be correct if presentation were dominated by pleuritic chest pain, focal hypoxia, or RV strain on POCUS (THANZ; eTG).
rationales.C: She is afebrile with no productive cough or focal consolidation; CXR pattern (upper lobe diversion, B-lines) and BNP favour congestion not infection. Antibiotics in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema are antibiotic stewardship failures. Severe CAP empirical therapy would be correct with fever, focal consolidation, raised inflammatory markers, and absent congestion (eTG Respiratory).
rationales.D: The minor troponin elevation is stable and likely demand-related from rapid AF and decompensated HF; there is no ECG evidence of STEMI and no NSTEMI dynamic rise/fall pattern. Type 2 MI from demand ischaemia is managed by treating the underlying decompensation. PPCI is correct only with STEMI (or equivalent) ECG (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS).
rationales.E: Shotgun therapy without diagnostic clarity exposes her to harm: broad antibiotics in non-infection, additional anticoagulation on therapeutic apixaban risks bleeding. The anti-pattern is treating uncertainty with combination empiricism rather than disambiguation. Combination therapy is correct only when truly undifferentiated and bridging is essential (e.g., undifferentiated shock with sepsis and PE both plausible) (Heart Foundation/CSANZ HF; eTG).

rationales.A: Sulfonylureas lower HbA1c but carry hypoglycaemia risk, do not improve cardiorenal outcomes, and offer no benefit in HFrEF or CKD progression. The anti-pattern is reaching for legacy HbA1c-only agents in a patient with cardiorenal indications. Gliclazide would be reasonable in T2DM without HFrEF/CKD when cost is constraining or as adjunct to glucose lowering (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Position Statement on T2DM).
rationales.C: Basal insulin lowers HbA1c but provides no cardiovascular outcome benefit and would be premature when oral and injectable non-insulin options remain. Insulin is correct when patient is glucotoxic (HbA1c >10%, ketonaemia, marked symptoms) or after exhausting oral/GLP-1 RA options (ADS-ADEA Algorithm).
rationales.D: DPP4 inhibitors are weight-neutral, well tolerated, but cardiorenally neutral. Saxagliptin specifically increases HF hospitalisation (SAVOR-TIMI 53). The anti-pattern is choosing weight neutrality over outcome benefit. DPP4 inhibitors are reasonable as third-line in absence of HFrEF/CKD where SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA are contraindicated (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; AMH).
rationales.E: Thiazolidinediones cause fluid retention via PPAR-gamma sodium reabsorption and are contraindicated in HFrEF (NYHA III/IV). Pioglitazone has glucose-lowering and modest cardiovascular benefits in selected patients, but never with established HFrEF (AMH; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

rationales.A: Premixed biphasic insulin covers basal and prandial but has the highest hypoglycaemia risk and least flexibility. This would be correct only when adherence to multi-injection regimens is poor and structured meal patterns are reliable; not first-line for isolated fasting hyperglycaemia (RACGP 2024).
rationales.B: Basal-bolus is reserved for failure of basal alone or significant post-prandial excursions; this patient has isolated fasting hyperglycaemia. The anti-pattern is starting with maximal complexity when minimal intervention suffices. Basal-bolus is correct after basal optimisation if post-prandial glucose remains uncontrolled (RACGP 2024).
rationales.D: NPH has a 4 to 8 hour peak with higher nocturnal hypoglycaemia risk than long-acting analogues; long-acting analogues (glargine, detemir, degludec) are preferred especially in older patients fearful of hypoglycaemia. NPH may be acceptable on cost grounds or in patients without nocturnal hypoglycaemia concern (RACGP 2024).
rationales.E: Prandial-only insulin addresses post-prandial excursions, not fasting hyperglycaemia, and would miss her dominant abnormality. The anti-pattern is matching insulin profile to the wrong glycaemic pattern. Prandial-only would be correct with isolated post-prandial spikes and normal fasting glucose (RACGP 2024).

rationales.B: Starting insulin before volume restoration worsens hypotension, accelerates intracellular potassium shift, and risks cardiovascular collapse. The anti-pattern is leading with insulin in shocked DKA. Fluid-then-insulin is the correct sequence; insulin can start within 1 hour of fluid if K>3.5 (eTG Endocrinology; JBDS DKA 2023).
rationales.C: Bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA except in severe acidaemia with pH <6.9 or life-threatening hyperkalaemia; routine use worsens intracellular acidosis and risks cerebral oedema (a key pre-2010 anti-pattern eliminated by current guidelines). Bicarbonate is correct only at pH <6.9 with the strict JBDS regimen (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.D: Potassium replacement is withheld while serum K is above 5.5 mmol/L; her K is 5.4 mmol/L (within range to start insulin). Insulin will shift K intracellularly, so K replacement is added once K falls below 5.5. Pre-emptive K bolus risks hyperkalaemic arrhythmia (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.E: Restarting basal insulin within the first 12 to 24 hours of DKA is recommended (JBDS subcut overlap strategy) but does NOT replace IV insulin infusion in established DKA and does not address the priority of volume resuscitation in shock. Subcut basal is correct as adjunct, not as sole therapy (JBDS DKA 2023).

rationales.A: Excessive fluid administration is a major contributor to cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA; the appropriate response to suspected cerebral oedema is to HALVE or pause maintenance fluids, not increase them. This is one of the most dangerous anti-patterns in DKA management (BSPED/RCH CPG DKA).
rationales.C: Stopping insulin allows ketogenesis to resume and does not treat rising ICP; insulin should be continued (often reduced) while neuroprotective measures are delivered. The anti-pattern is removing the etiologic treatment. Insulin can be reduced (e.g., to 0.05 units/kg/hr) but not stopped (RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.D: Glucose 11 mmol/L is not hypoglycaemic; when DKA glucose falls below 14 mmol/L, the correct action is to ADD 5 or 10% dextrose to the maintenance fluid rather than push concentrated dextrose. The anti-pattern is treating any low-normal sugar as hypoglycaemia in a critically ill patient (RCH CPG: DKA).
rationales.E: While headache and drowsiness raise meningitis as a differential, the pattern of Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia) plus timing in DKA make cerebral oedema overwhelmingly likely. Empirical antibiotics should not delay osmotherapy; meningitis workup is correct only after stabilisation if features persist (RCH CPG: DKA).

rationales.A: Severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) occurs in both HHS and DKA; glucose level alone does not discriminate. The anti-pattern is using a number that is shared between the two diagnoses to differentiate. HHS typically has higher glucose (often >40) but overlap with severe DKA is substantial (eTG Endocrinology - HHS).
rationales.C: AKI is common in both DKA and HHS due to volume depletion and does not differentiate. This is the classic shared-feature anti-pattern. AKI severity may favour HHS due to more prolonged osmotic diuresis but is not discriminatory (KDIGO; eTG).
rationales.D: Infection precipitates both HHS and DKA; the precipitant cannot discriminate the metabolic phenotype. The discrimination is biochemical (ketosis and acidosis present in DKA, absent or minimal in HHS), not aetiological (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.E: Drowsiness can occur in both, although depressed consciousness correlates more closely with osmolality (and therefore is more often severe in HHS). However the DEFINING feature remains the biochemistry pattern (ketones, pH, osmolality), not GCS alone. GCS is supportive but not the specific discriminator (JBDS HHS 2022).

rationales.A: Insulin glargine alone is unlikely to cause this severity in a previously stable patient; reflexive dose reduction without identifying precipitants misses the actual cause. The anti-pattern is treating insulin as default culprit when sulfonylurea-antibiotic interaction is present. Glargine dose review is appropriate after identifying and addressing the precipitant (RACGP 2024; AMH).
rationales.C: Metformin does not cause hypoglycaemia in monotherapy and only minimally contributes in combination therapy because it does not stimulate insulin secretion. The anti-pattern is blaming the most familiar drug. Metformin contributes to hypoglycaemia only in extreme overdose or via lactic acidosis indirectly (AMH).
rationales.D: Cefalexin is not a recognised cause of hypoglycaemia. Among antibiotics, fluoroquinolones (gatifloxacin most strongly) and trimethoprim are the main offenders potentiating sulfonylureas. Cefalexin causes hypoglycaemia only through reduced oral intake from nausea, not pharmacologically (AMH).
rationales.E: 1 mg IM glucagon is the standard adult dose and her glucose responded - this is not the cause of the original event. The anti-pattern is blaming the rescue rather than the precipitant. Glucagon dose review is appropriate only when initial response fails or in glycogen-depleted states (alcohol, prolonged fasting) (ANZCOR; AMH).

rationales.B: Amiodarone is 37% iodine by weight and PROVIDES the iodine substrate that worsens thyroid storm via the Jod-Basedow effect; it is contraindicated in thyrotoxicosis. Beta blockade (propranolol, esmolol) is preferred for rate control in storm. Amiodarone is a classic 'wrong drug at the wrong time' anti-pattern in thyroid emergency (AMH).
rationales.C: The clinical picture fits thyroid storm with a clear precipitant (medication non-adherence). Empirical antimicrobials may be reasonable if infection is also suspected but they do not address the thyroid emergency. Antibiotics first would be correct only if sepsis were the dominant working diagnosis with a separately identified source (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.D: Myxoedema coma presents with hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, and depressed consciousness - the OPPOSITE picture (hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, tremor here). The anti-pattern is reflexively reaching for thyroid hormone in any thyroid emergency. T4 + steroid is correct only in confirmed myxoedema coma (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.E: Thyroid storm carries 10 to 30% mortality without aggressive inpatient management; discharge is unsafe regardless of antithyroid drug. The anti-pattern is matching dose to thyrotoxicosis (which would be appropriate for ambulatory severe Graves) rather than to the storm phenotype. Outpatient carbimazole is correct for stable Graves disease, never for storm (eTG Endocrinology).

rationales.A: RAI can WORSEN active Graves orbitopathy (especially moderate-severe disease, smoking history, high TRAb), and pregnancy must be avoided for 6 months post-RAI - conflicting with her conception plans. RAI would be correct in Graves disease without orbitopathy, not pursuing pregnancy within 6 months, and no large goitre (ESA/ETA 2021).
rationales.C: Long-term low-dose carbimazole is an emerging option for selected patients (with mild disease, low TRAb, no relapse history) but is less effective at definitive cure; high TRAb plus relapse history plus active orbitopathy favour definitive treatment, and carbimazole is teratogenic in early pregnancy. Long-term carbimazole is correct only in mild persistent disease where definitive therapy is declined (eTG; AMH).
rationales.D: PTU carries higher risk of severe hepatotoxicity than carbimazole and is reserved for first trimester pregnancy (when carbimazole has higher teratogenicity) or thyroid storm. Indefinite PTU is the wrong long-term agent. PTU is correct in first trimester or storm only (AMH).
rationales.E: IV methylprednisolone treats active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy but does NOT treat the underlying hyperthyroidism; it complements but does not replace definitive thyroid management. Methylprednisolone is correct as adjunctive therapy for orbitopathy (alongside definitive thyroid treatment), not as monotherapy (ETA 2021).

rationales.A: Oral therapy is correct for asymptomatic mild hypocalcaemia or as maintenance after IV correction. Symptomatic acute hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV calcium for prompt myocardial protection. Oral calcium + calcitriol is the maintenance pathway after IV stabilisation (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.C: Calcium chloride contains 3x more elemental calcium per mL but is highly irritant and risks severe extravasation injury via peripheral line; calcium gluconate is preferred for peripheral administration. Calcium chloride is correct only via central venous access or in cardiac arrest (AMH; eTG).
rationales.D: Magnesium 0.74 mmol/L is borderline and replacement is appropriate (low Mg perpetuates hypocalcaemia by impairing PTH release and action), but Mg alone will not correct her acute symptomatic hypocalcaemia in the required timeframe. Mg-alone is correct in pure hypomagnesaemic hypocalcaemia with mild symptoms (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.E: Cinacalcet is a calcimimetic that LOWERS calcium in hyperparathyroidism; it is contraindicated in hypocalcaemia and would worsen her presentation - a classic wrong-direction-drug anti-pattern. Cinacalcet is correct in primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism, never in hypocalcaemia (AMH).

rationales.B: Insulin-dextrose shifts K intracellularly and is part of the standard regimen, but it is given AFTER calcium when ECG changes are present; insulin does NOT stabilise the myocardium and takes 15 to 30 minutes for peak effect. The anti-pattern is starting potassium shift therapy before membrane stabilisation when ECG is abnormal (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.C: Beta-2 agonists shift K intracellularly and complement insulin-dextrose, but again do not stabilise the myocardium and act in 15 to 30 minutes. The anti-pattern is using a slower-onset shift agent ahead of immediate myocardial protection. Nebulised salbutamol is appropriate as part of combined K-shift therapy after calcium (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.D: Cation exchange resins remove K from the body but act over hours; they are useful for ongoing removal alongside dialysis planning. The anti-pattern is using a slow-acting agent in an ECG emergency. Resonium is correct as adjunct for ongoing removal after immediate threats addressed (eTG Endocrinology).
rationales.E: Dialysis is definitive for refractory hyperkalaemia or fluid overload but takes time to arrange; emergency medical therapy (calcium, insulin-dextrose, salbutamol) must not be delayed for imaging or vascular access. The anti-pattern is letting definitive intervention delay emergency stabilisation (eTG Endocrinology).

rationales.A: Fluid restriction is correct for SIADH (euvolaemic hyponatraemia); this patient is hypovolaemic with polyuria, weight loss, and postural hypotension after SAH. Restricting fluid would worsen cerebral hypoperfusion and the risk of delayed cerebral ischaemia (DCI). The anti-pattern is treating any post-neurosurgical hyponatraemia as SIADH without volume assessment (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS 2023).
rationales.C: Tolvaptan (V2 antagonist) treats SIADH by increasing free water clearance; it would worsen hypovolaemic hyponatraemia in CSW and is contraindicated in volume depletion. The anti-pattern is using SIADH-specific therapy without confirming the volume state. Tolvaptan is correct only in confirmed euvolaemic SIADH (AMH).
rationales.D: Hypertonic saline bolus is correct for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, coma) for a target rise of 4 to 6 mmol/L in 1 to 2 hours, but a single bolus without ongoing maintenance volume in CSW under-treats volume depletion and risks overshoot. Hypertonic saline is correct as part of CSW management when sodium fails to rise on isotonic saline, not as sole therapy (ENLS 2023).
rationales.E: Free water in 5% dextrose worsens hyponatraemia and is contraindicated; in any neurocritical patient it also increases intracranial water content. This is a major anti-pattern in neurocritical care. Dextrose is appropriate only for documented hypoglycaemia (ENLS 2023).

rationales.A: IV thrombolysis is contraindicated within 48 hours of a DOAC dose unless DOAC levels are confirmed sub-therapeutic (anti-Xa for apixaban). His last apixaban was at 0700, well within 48 hours. The anti-pattern is reflex thrombolysis in any LVO without checking anticoagulant timing. Thrombolysis is correct only when DOAC is excluded or levels are sub-therapeutic (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).
rationales.B: Pre-thrombolysis BP target is <185/110 mmHg; for thrombectomy without thrombolysis higher BP is tolerated (typically <220/120). Aggressive lowering to <140/90 risks penumbral hypoperfusion and infarct extension. Tight BP control is correct only POST-thrombectomy with successful recanalisation (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS).
rationales.D: Antiplatelet alone is not the reperfusion treatment for a proximal LVO; thrombectomy is the standard of care. Antiplatelet within 24 hours of thrombolysis would also be contraindicated. Aspirin-only is correct only for ischaemic stroke without LVO and outside reperfusion windows (Australian Stroke Foundation).
rationales.E: Idarucizumab reverses DABIGATRAN, not apixaban. Andexanet alfa partially reverses factor Xa inhibitors but is expensive, not universally available, and not routinely used to enable thrombolysis (risk of rebound). Direct transfer to thrombectomy avoids the reversal pitfall. The anti-pattern is conflating the two DOAC reversal agents (AMH; Australian Stroke Foundation).

rationales.B: Antiepileptic monotherapy after a single unprovoked seizure with no abnormalities is not routinely recommended; the modest reduction in recurrence is offset by drug burden. 1-month suspension is well short of the Austroads minimum (6 months private). Valproate is teratogenic and inappropriate in a woman of reproductive age without explicit pre-conception counselling. Multiple errors (eTG Neurology; Austroads).
rationales.C: There are no features of meningitis (no fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered consciousness); LP is not indicated for an isolated first seizure. The anti-pattern is overinvestigating in low-pretest-probability scenarios. LP is correct with fever, headache, or focal neurology suggestive of CNS infection (eTG Antibiotic).
rationales.D: Continuous video-EEG is for diagnostic uncertainty (PNES vs epilepsy) or pre-surgical evaluation, not standard first-seizure workup. The anti-pattern is escalating to specialised testing before standard workup. Routine outpatient EEG (with sleep deprivation if first study negative) is the standard first investigation (eTG Neurology).
rationales.E: Commercial driving requires longer abstinence under Austroads AFTD: typically 10 years seizure-free off medication, or 5 years on medication with strict conditions. Immediate return is unsafe and legally non-compliant. The treating doctor must specifically document commercial-vs-private status (Austroads AFTD 2022).

rationales.A: Two adequate benzodiazepine doses have already been given with no response; further benzodiazepine risks respiratory depression without added benefit. The anti-pattern is repeating first-line when escalation is mandated. Escalate to second-line antiepileptic (levetiracetam, phenytoin, or valproate) (eTG Neurology; ANZCOR).
rationales.C: Anaesthetic induction is third-line, after a second-line antiepileptic has failed; jumping to anaesthesia skips a step and commits to ICU intubation unnecessarily. Propofol induction is correct in confirmed refractory status epilepticus (failure of second-line agent) (eTG Neurology).
rationales.D: Oral phenytoin via NG tube has unpredictable absorption and is too slow for acute status; IV phenytoin (20 mg/kg, max rate 50 mg/min) is the parenteral option if levetiracetam is not chosen. The anti-pattern is using oral route in an emergency. NG phenytoin is correct only for outpatient initiation or maintenance, not acute status (eTG Neurology; AMH).
rationales.E: Empirical glucose is reserved for hypoglycaemia; his glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. Thiamine is appropriate if alcohol use disorder is suspected (always BEFORE glucose to prevent Wernicke precipitation) but does not terminate the seizure. Thiamine + glucose is correct in suspected alcohol-related seizure with hypoglycaemia (eTG Neurology).
